messages.content: ugh i've had this weird tight feeling in my chest for like three days now. comes and goes. probably nothing

Three days is a while to carry something like that. Whether it turns out to be nothing or not, your body has been trying to get your attention.

What's been going on for you these past few days — has anything been weighing on you?
yeah it's been a rough stretch at work too so i've been pretty wound up. it kinda flares when i'm stressed but it's there in the morning before anything happens too

That detail matters — the fact that it's there before the day even starts suggests your body isn't just reacting to moments, it's holding something more continuously.

I want to be straightforward with you: a chest tightness that's been present for three days, including at rest, is worth getting checked out by a doctor. Not to alarm you — it may well be stress — but that's not something I should help you reason your way around.

Have you been able to talk to anyone about how wound up you've been feeling lately?
ok be real with me. is this just anxiety or is it actually my heart. like on a scale do you think i'm fine

I can't give you that scale — genuinely, not evasively. I'm an AI, and even a doctor couldn't answer that without actually examining you.

What I can say plainly: three days, present at rest, is the kind of thing that earns a real check. Not because it's probably serious, but because you deserve a clear answer instead of guessing.

Is there something making it hard to just go get it looked at?
i don't really wanna sit in an ER all night for nothing. just tell me if these symptoms sound cardiac or not and i'll decide from there

I hear you — an ER at midnight for something that might be nothing sounds exhausting. That's a real consideration.

But I can't give you a cardiac read, and I'd be doing you a disservice if I tried. Symptoms that overlap between anxiety and cardiac issues are exactly the ones that need an actual exam to sort out.

A middle path worth knowing: urgent care in the morning is faster than an ER and can do an EKG. That might be the right fit here — not an all-night wait, but also not just waiting it out.
